title: Ensure that the --use-service-account-credentials argument is set to true

input_row.title: Ensure that the --use-service-account-credentials argument is set to true
input_row.description: Use individual service account credentials for each controller.
input_row.rationale: The controller manager creates a service account per controller in the kube-system namespace, generates a credential for it, and builds a dedicated API client with that service account credential for each controller loop to use. Setting the --use-service- account-credentials to true runs each control loop within the controller manager using a separate service account credential. When used in combination with RBAC, this ensures that the control loops run with the minimum permissions required to perform their intended tasks. Impact: Whatever authorizer is configured for the cluster, it must grant sufficient permissions to the service accounts to perform their intended tasks. When using the RBAC authorizer, those roles are created and bound to the appropriate service accounts in the kube- system namespace automatically with default roles and rolebindings that are auto- reconciled on startup. If using other authorization methods (ABAC, Webhook, etc), the cluster deployer is responsible for granting appropriate permissions to the service accounts (the required permissions can be seen by inspecting the controller-roles.yaml and controller- role-bindings.yaml files for the RBAC roles.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the Control Plane node: ps -ef | grep kube-controller-manager Verify that the --use-service-account-credentials argument is set to true.
input_row.remediation: Edit the Controller Manager pod specification file /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube- controller-manager.yaml on the Control Plane node to set the below parameter. --use-service-account-credentials=true Default Value: By default, --use-service-account-credentials is set to false. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-controller-manager/ 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/service-accounts-admin/ 3. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release- 1.6/plugin/pkg/auth/authorizer/rbac/bootstrappolicy/testdata/controller-roles.yaml 4. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release- 1.6/plugin/pkg/auth/authorizer/rbac/bootstrappolicy/testdata/controller-role- bindings.yaml 5. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/authorization/rbac/#controller-roles
input_row.profile_applicability: •  Level 1 - Master Node •  Level 2 - Master Node
input_row.impact: Whatever authorizer is configured for the cluster, it must grant sufficient permissions to the service accounts to perform their intended tasks. When using the RBAC authorizer, those roles are created and bound to the appropriate service accounts in the kube- system namespace automatically with default roles and rolebindings that are auto- reconciled on startup. If using other authorization methods (ABAC, Webhook, etc), the cluster deployer is responsible for granting appropriate permissions to the service accounts (the required permissions can be seen by inspecting the controller-roles.yaml and controller- role-bindings.yaml files for the RBAC roles.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_controller_manager_use_service_account_credentials_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Query the kube-controller-manager Pod specification, typically located at `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml` if running as a static Pod, or via the Kubernetes API if managed by a higher-level controller.
- Use the Kubernetes API or `kubectl` to retrieve the current configuration:
  - If static Pod: `kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-controller-manager -o yaml`
  - If managed by a Deployment/DaemonSet: `kubectl -n kube-system get deployment kube-controller-manager -o yaml`
- Inspect the `command` or `args` field of the kube-controller-manager container spec for the presence of the `--use-service-account-credentials=true` argument.
- Pass Criteria: The kube-controller-manager must be running with `--use-service-account-credentials=true` explicitly set.
- Fail Criteria: The argument is missing or set to `false`.
- Example check (using kubectl and jq for static Pod):
  ```
  kubectl -n kube-system get pod -l component=kube-controller-manager -o json | jq -r '.items[].spec.containers[].command[]' | grep -- '--use-service-account-credentials=true'
  ```
  or, for manifest file (if direct file access is available):
  ```
  grep -- '--use-service-account-credentials' /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml
  ```
- The check passes if the argument is present and set to `true` in the running configuration.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.7.1 PDF
